narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score of 38 and undergraduate GPA of 3.40, both of which stand above the typical student's marks. Your family's middle-income background and plan to attend full-time also align with the majority of applicants who succeed. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam outcomes based on profiles like yours, estimated a 65% chance you'll face failure on that critical test—high enough to lead to the tough decision to reject your application right now. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model a notable dip in later academic performance compared to peers. This outweighed positives like your LSAT, which strongly nudged the odds toward passing by exceeding the typical score of 36.7, and your GPA, which did the same in a smaller but helpful way. Your middle family income contributed somewhat toward the failure risk relative to higher brackets, while your Year 1 decile of 5 was only a minor push in that direction, and full-time status barely factored in favorably. Overall, your profile shows promise in entrance metrics but raises concerns about sustaining performance through later years, unlike typical admitted students whose rankings stay consistently higher. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year deciles, perhaps seeking targeted tutoring or mentorship tailored for Black women in law, and reapply once you've strengthened those areas. You've got resilience on your side; this is just one model's view, and your story is far from over. Keep pushing—you belong in this field.
